title: Legislation by Senator Brian W. Jones (R-Santee) to crackdown on the growing crime of catalytic converter theft was approved unanimously by the Senate Business, Professions and Eco

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "Catalytic converter theft in California continues to rise.",
    "There are few risks of getting caught or prosecuted for catalytic converter theft.",
    "Senate Bill 919 will assist in tracking and identifying stolen catalytic converters.",
    "New and used motor vehicle dealers will be required to permanently mark the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) on the catalytic converter of any vehicle before selling it.",
    "Metal recyclers will only be allowed to purchase catalytic converters that have a clearly visible and untampered VIN.",
    "Metal recyclers will have to keep detailed records of who sold them each specific catalytic converter and make those records accessible to law enforcement.",
    "The current loose practice of selling and buying catalytic converters will be discouraged by the new legislation.",
    "Stolen catalytic converters can bring a thief up to $250.",
    "Replacing a stolen catalytic converter can cost a motorist up to $4,000.",
    "The theft of an under-vehicle catalytic converter can be completed in less than two minutes using basic tools such as a pipe wrench or cordless Sawzall.",
    "More catalytic converter thefts occur in California than any other state."
  ]
}
```
